items.ocr_text:
The booklets produced by a certain publisher contain, on average, 1 incorrect letter per 30 000 letters, and these errors occur randomly. A randomly chosen booklet from this publisher contains 12 500 letters.

Use a suitable approximating distribution to find the probability that this booklet contains at least 2 errors.

evidence: Question number '1' printed at left margin
Full question stem and instruction present
Marks bracket '[3]' visible

items.ocr_text:
2 Lengths of a certain species of lizard are known to be normally distributed with standard deviation 3.2 cm. A naturalist measures the lengths of a random sample of 100 lizards of this species and obtains an α% confidence interval for the population mean. He finds that the total width of this interval is 1.25 cm.

Find α.

evidence: Printed question number '2' at start of page
Full question statement and instruction 'Find α.' present
Mark allocation '[5]' visible

items.ocr_text:
3 In the past, the mean time taken by Freda for a particular daily journey was 39.2 minutes. Following the introduction of a one-way system, Freda wishes to test whether the mean time for the journey has decreased. She notes the times, t minutes, for 40 randomly chosen journeys and summarises the results as follows.

n = 40    Σt = 1504    Σt² = 57 760

(a) Calculate unbiased estimates of the population mean and variance of the new journey time. [3]

(b) Test, at the 5% significance level, whether the population mean time has decreased. [5]

evidence: Printed question number '3' at top-left
Text begins with 'In the past...', no prior continuation markers

items.ocr_text:
4 The number of accidents on a certain road has a Poisson distribution with mean 0.4 per 50-day period.
(a) Find the probability that there will be fewer than 3 accidents during a year (365 days).
(b) The probability that there will be no accidents during a period of n days is greater than 0.95.
Find the largest possible value of n.

evidence: Printed question number '4' at start of page
Subpart (a) clearly labeled and complete statement
Subpart (b) follows (a) without new question number
Same question context (Poisson accidents)
No indication of question closure

items.ocr_text:
5 Bottles of Lanta contain approximately 300 ml of juice. The volume of juice, in millilitres, in a bottle is 300 + X, where X is a random variable with probability density function given by

f(x) = { 3/4000 (100 - x^2)   -10 ≤ x ≤ 10,
         0                  otherwise.

(a) Find the probability that a randomly chosen bottle of Lanta contains more than 305 ml of juice. [3]

(b) Given that 25% of bottles of Lanta contain more than (300 + p) ml of juice, show that
p^3 - 300p + 1000 = 0. [4]
(c) Given that p = 3.47, and that 50% of bottles of Lanta contain between (300 − q) and (300 + q) ml of juice, find q. Justify your answer.

evidence: Printed question number '5' at top-left
Subparts (a) and (b) present
No prior question visible on this page
Subpart label '(c)' follows previous subpart '(b)' from carry state

items.ocr_text:
6 The volumes, in millilitres, of large and small cups of tea are modelled by the distributions N(200, 30) and N(110, 20) respectively.

(a) Find the probability that the total volume of a randomly chosen large cup of tea and a randomly chosen small cup of tea is less than 300 ml.
(b) Find the probability that the volume of a randomly chosen large cup of tea is more than twice the volume of a randomly chosen small cup of tea.

items.ocr_text:
7 A national survey shows that 95% of year 12 students use social media. Arvin suspects that the percentage of year 12 students at his college who use social media is less than the national percentage. He chooses a random sample of 20 students at his college and notes the number who use social media. He then carries out a test at the 2% significance level.

(a) Find the rejection region for the test.
(b) Find the probability of a Type I error.
(c) Jimmy believes that the true percentage at Arvin's college is 70%. Assuming that Jimmy is correct, find the probability of a Type II error.
